MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANTS' MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION

Appearances: Marissa Dennis of Allen Matkins Leck Gamble Mallory & Natsis (213.622.5555) for defendants and moving parties; Shauna Woods (via Zoom) of Allen Matkins Leck Gamble Mallory & Natsis (213.622.5555) for defendants and moving parties; John O'Connor (via Zoom) of O'Connor and Associates (415.693.9960) for plaintiffs and responding parties.

Argued and the Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: GRANTED in part and DENIED in part. Neither party requested permission to file oversized briefs despite this motion being continued twice. Therefore, excessive briefs filed by both parties are stricken although considered.

The motion is granted as to the First cause of action for Breach of Contract based on lack of consideration. The benefit Plaintiff-promisee conferred on Defendant-promisor of not challenging Defendant's entitlements was not bargained for as the exchange for the promise to maintain level rent. (Property California SCJLW One Corp. v. Leamy (2018) 25 Cal.App.5th 1155, 1165; Def.'s UMF, No. 7; Dennis Decl., Exh. W, p. 226:24-228:20.) The motion is denied on all other grounds.

The motion is denied as to the Second Cause of Action for Fraud and Deceit. There is a triable issue of fact whether Plaintiffs relied on the October 2014 Promise, and whether that reliance was reasonable. Plaintiff sufficiently alleges the harm and the Economic Loss Rule does not bar any of Plaintiff's claims. (Erlich v. Menezes (1999) 21 Cal.4th 543, 553-554; see also Harris v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (1993) 14 Cal.App.4th 70, 78.)

The motion is denied as to the Third Cause of Action for Promissory Estoppel. There is a triable issue of fact whether there was a clear promise, whether Plaintiffs relied on that promise, and whether reliance was reasonable.

The motion is granted as to the Fourth Cause of Action for Breach of Warranty of Authority. This claim requires the existence of a contract. (See Jeppi v. Brockman Holding Co. (1949) 34 Cal.2d 11, 18-19; CC Section 2343.)

The motion is moot as to the declaratory relief. Plaintiffs are no longer in possession of the Premises and will dismiss their claim.

The motion for summary adjudication as to the claims against John Kilroy in his individual capacity is denied. There is a triable issue of fact of whether Mr. Kilroy participated, authorized, or directed the alleged fraud.

Plaintiff's claims are not barred by Res Judicata. Claim preclusion arises if a second suit involves: (1) the same cause of action (2) between the same parties (3) after a final judgment on the merits in the first suit. (Mycogen Corp. v. Monsanto Co. (2002) 28 Cal.4th 888, 896.) Defendant fails to establish prong 3. The arbitrator only had authority to determine the Fair Market Rent of the lease option. (Plfs. Evidence Ex. 1 at 2 Ex. 1.1.).

Prevailing party to submit a proposed form of order. Judge: Charles F. Haines, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Reporter: Jane Hong, CSR 11975 (T: 909.831.2644, E: csrjane@hotmail.com) (501/CFH)
